coredata.dc:title: Sustainable fluorescent carbon dots derived from sprouted palmyra seeds and their utilization in Fe3+ and ferritin detection in aqueous medium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kathiravan, Arunkumar
Ilango, Bhuvaneesh
Jhonsi, Mariadoss Asha
coredata.dc:description: The effective utilization of biomass as a sustainable and renewable resource for producing luminous carbon dots is a current research priority. Germinated palmyra seeds has been utilized as the carbon precursor for the first time through hydrothermal methods to produce fluorescent carbon dots. A comprehensive investigation of the prepared carbon dots has been done using sophisticated microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The prepared CDs displayed a particle size of 3–4 nm, exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology as seen in SEM and HR-TEM images. The synthesized carbon dots had exceptional water solubility, displayed excitation wavelength-dependent emission, and possessed a high fluorescence quantum yield of 12 %. The functional groups of the CDs were examined by FTIR spectroscopy which demonstrates that the CDs were inherently functionalized with –OH, N–H, and CO groups. Owing to their pronounced fluorescence properties and specific functional groups, the CDs are effectively utilized for sensing investigations. The CDs exhibited a highly selective response to Fe3+ ions compared to other analytes, with a detection limit of 1.42 nM. The time-resolved fluorescence quenching data were analyzed to elucidate the sensing mechanism, which has been found to be photoinduced electron transfer from excited CDs to Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, as-prepared CDs were effectively employed to detect Fe3+ ions in actual water samples, validating their advantageous application. On the other hand, the highly sensitive nature of CDs has been utilized to detect Fe3+ in ferritin protein, yielding satisfactory results. Overall, this study demonstrates that sprouted palmyra seeds are effective in generating green, cost-efficient carbon dots as a promising fluorescent probe for the sensitive, swift, and selective detection of Fe3+ in water-based environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass
Carbon dots
Sensor
Fe3+ ions
Ferritin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for the SEED research grant (VTU/Seed Fund/FY 2023\u20132024/014). A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for the SEED research grant (VTU/Seed Fund/FY 2023-2024/014). A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM and (b) HR-TEM image and (c) particle size distribution of palmyra-derived CDs.
(a) Raman and (b) FTIR spectra of palmyra derived CDs.
(a) UV-Vis absorption spectra, (b) excitation dependent fluorescence spectra and (c) fluorescence decay of palmyra derived CDs in aqueous solution at natural pH.
Effect of (a) pH, (b) ionic strength and (c) storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs.
(a) Selectivity plot of CDs towards diverse metal ions (b) interference plot of CDs in presence of various metal ions (where A = Fe2+, B = Cu2+, C = Zn2+, D = Cd2+, E = Hg2+, F = Mn2+, and G = Pb2+).

(a) Effect of Fe3+ ions (0.3–15 µM) on the fluorescence spectra of CDs (b) a plot of change in fluorescence intensity vs Fe3+ ions concentrations (c) Stern-Volmer plot and (d) modified Stern-Volmer plot of CDs.
(a) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of CDs with and without the addition of Fe3+ ions (b) graphical illustration of the possible interaction between the CDs and Fe3+ ions.

(a) Selectivity and quenching efficiency of CDs with different proteins at pH 6, (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of ferritin and (c) plot of change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs with different concentrations of ferritin at pH 6.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of CDs from germinated palmyra seeds.
Graphical illustration of the possible interaction between the CDs and ferritin.
Comparison of LOD values of biomass derived CDs towards Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium.
Detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sustainable fluorescent carbon dots derived from sprouted palmyra seeds and their utilization in Fe3+ and ferritin detection in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600 062, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry & Centre for Molecular Photoscience, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai – 600 062, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600 048, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, B. S. Abdur Rahman Crescent Institute of Science and Technology, Chennai-600 048, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective utilization of biomass as a sustainable and renewable resource for producing luminous carbon dots is a current research priority. Germinated palmyra seeds has been utilized as the carbon precursor for the first time through hydrothermal methods to produce fluorescent carbon dots. A comprehensive investigation of the prepared carbon dots has been done using sophisticated microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. The prepared CDs displayed a particle size of 3–4 nm, exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology as seen in SEM and HR-TEM images. The synthesized carbon dots had exceptional water solubility, displayed excitation wavelength-dependent emission, and possessed a high fluorescence quantum yield of 12 %. The functional groups of the CDs were examined by FTIR spectroscopy which demonstrates that the CDs were inherently functionalized with –OH, N–H, and CO groups. Owing to their pronounced fluorescence properties and specific functional groups, the CDs are effectively utilized for sensing investigations. The CDs exhibited a highly selective response to Fe3+ ions compared to other analytes, with a detection limit of 1.42 nM. The time-resolved fluorescence quenching data were analyzed to elucidate the sensing mechanism, which has been found to be photoinduced electron transfer from excited CDs to Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, as-prepared CDs were effectively employed to detect Fe3+ ions in actual water samples, validating their advantageous application. On the other hand, the highly sensitive nature of CDs has been utilized to detect Fe3+ in ferritin protein, yielding satisfactory results. Overall, this study demonstrates that sprouted palmyra seeds are effective in generating green, cost-efficient carbon dots as a promising fluorescent probe for the sensitive, swift, and selective detection of Fe3+ in water-based environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass, as a renewable and copious resource, provides a sustainable remedy to numerous environmental issues while supporting circular economy concepts [1]. Agroforestry waste constitutes a substantial fraction of biomass that, if neglected, results in environmental damage, encompassing greenhouse gas emissions, water pollution, and soil degradation [2]. The transformation of this waste into value-added products not only alleviates these problems but also fosters environmentally sustainable alternatives to traditional synthetic materials. A possible method of valorization is the transformation of biomass into carbonaceous nanomaterials, particularly carbon dots (CDs) [3–8]. Since the accidental discovery of CDs by Li et al. in 2004 [9], these zero-dimensional nanomaterials have garnered significant attention owing to their remarkable features, such as photoluminescence, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high water solubility [10,11]. CDs are typically synthesized using two approaches namely top-down and bottom-up, wherein biomass derived CDs are preferably synthesized by employing various bottom-up techniques including hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, pyrolysis, ultrasonication and so on [12,13]. These characteristics make biomass-derived CDs exceptionally appealing for diverse applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and environmental sensing [14–17].

The use of agricultural waste for the synthesis of CDs offers a twin advantage: transforming waste into functional materials and diminishing dependence on costly and potentially hazardous chemical precursors. Recently, multiple studies have evidenced the effective synthesis of CDs from diverse biomass sources, including fruit peels [18], leaves [19], and vegetable waste [20]. Among diverse biomass sources, Palmyra (Borassus flabellifer), a tropical plant indigenous to South Asia, possesses unexploited potential for CDs synthesis. The germinated seeds of Palmyra, historically utilized in medicine, are abundant in carbohydrates, proteins, and medicinal substances [21,22]. The germination process boosts the functional groups on the seed surface [23], rendering them an exceptional precursor for the synthesis of CDs. Despite this potential, there are no reported instances of the development of CDs from sprouted palmyra seeds, offering an innovative chance to investigate a bio-based and sustainable fluorescent material for environmental sensing applications.

Biomass-derived CDs exhibit significant potential in environmental pollution control, especially for the detection of metal ions in aquatic systems [24,25]. Iron (Fe³⁺) is an ion of paramount significance in human health and environment. Despite the essential role of the Fe3+ ion as a trace element in various biological processes [26], its overaccumulation in aquatic environments poses considerable environmental and health hazards. Elevated Fe3+ concentrations, frequently caused by industrial effluents and agricultural runoff, may result in significant health complications including anemia, Parkinson's disease [27], and organ failure [28]. Thus, the World Health Organization (WHO) has set a recommendation limit of 0.3 mg/L for Fe3+ in potable water to alleviate these concerns [29]. Conventional methods for detecting Fe3+ ions, including atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), electrochemical sensors, and various chromatographic techniques, although sensitive and accurate, are frequently time-consuming, costly, and necessitate intricate instrumentation [30]. Recently, fluorescence-based chemosensors have attracted considerable interest due to their rapid reaction, cost-effectiveness, and remarkable sensitivity in metal ion detection. CDs, due to their distinctive optical characteristics, offer a suitable platform for these applications. Their hydrophilic nature and stability under diverse environmental circumstances render them appropriate for sensing in aquatic environments.

In the present investigation, water-soluble CDs from sprouted palmyra seeds were developed using a simple, one-pot hydrothermal method. The CDs were comprehensively characterized employing essential techniques and utilized for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous solution. This study not only emphasizes the need of using a sustainable biomass resource, but it also shows the potential of biomass-derived CDs in solving pertinent environmental challenges, such as metal ion contamination in water systems.

The palmyra seeds utilized in this study were procured from rural regions in Tamil Nadu, India. All the necessary starting materials and reagents for synthesis and spectral analysis were acquired from commercial sources (Sigma-Aldrich, Avra Chemicals, and TCI Chemicals, India). The size and shape of the synthesized carbon dots were examined leveraging high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Philips Tecnai F20 G2 FEI-TEM). CDs were deposited onto a carbon-coated copper grid by dropping a diluted solution, which was subsequently allowed to air-dry, resulting in a thin film suitable for TEM analysis. SEM measurements were conducted utilizing the EI Quanta FEG 200. The FTIR spectrum of CDs was obtained using a JASCO FT-IR ATR 6300 spectrometer at room temperature within the spectral region of 4000–400 cm−1. The UV-Visible absorption spectra of carbon dots were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence and sensing measurements were conducted utilizing a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer. Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) was utilized to analyze the fluorescence decay characteristics of carbon dots under 390 nm excitation. The excitation source has an instrument response function (IRF) of 0.1 ns, which is crucial for fluorescence decay analysis as it accounts for the time resolution and characteristics of the detection system used to measure the decay curve. In TCSPC, the IRF is typically obtained by recording the system’s response to a scattering standard or a short-lived fluorophore. This IRF is then applied in mathematical fitting or deconvolution algorithms to accurately determine the fluorescence lifetime from the measured decay curve. All analytical experiments were performed at a temperature of 273 K.

The germinated palmyra seeds were carefully separated from the sprouted nuts by de-husking them using a sharp blade. The soft, spongy endosperm was then thoroughly washed twice with deionized water to remove surface impurities. The cleaned endosperm was then finely ground using a kitchen blender. The resulting material was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 8 hours in a muffle furnace. After completion of the reaction, the autoclave was permitted to cool to room temperature. To purify the resulting product, a systematic purification process was employed to remove unreacted precursors, impurities, and by-products. First, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm, and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was subsequently washed with dichloromethane to remove traces of small organic moieties. The solution was further filtered through Whatman filter paper, followed by a 0.2 µm syringe filter, ensuring the removal of any residual particulates. Finally, the filtered solution underwent dialysis using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 kDa, yielding highly pure carbon dots with a uniform particle size of ∼10 nm.

A solution of quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent) is used as the reference for measuring the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. The solution has been prepared with absorbance values below 0.2 optical density to offset reabsorption effects. Consequently, the fluorescence intensities of the standard and CDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 366 nm. The formula described in equation below is employed to determine the quantum yields of CDs.
Φ
F
Φ
R
=
I
S
I
R
×
F
R
F
S
×
n
2
n
R
2
Where S stands for the quantum yield of the sample (CDs), R refers to the quantum yield of standard solution (0.54), I is the integrated peak area of the fluorescence spectrum, F for the fraction of light absorbed (1 −10−A) and η for the refractive index of the solvent.

To investigate the selectivity of the derived Palmyra CDs, a number of metal ions (Zn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, and Fe2+) at a concentration of 10 µM were introduced to a series of CDs solutions, facilitating the assessment of the specific interactions between the CDs and each metal ion. The fluorescence emission spectra of each sample solution were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, with emission monitored at 430 nm, allowing the determination of Fe3+ ion selectivity. In addition, the fluorescence titration approach was employed to assess the sensitivity of the CDs towards Fe3+ ions. In a typical experiment, the Fe3+ ion solution (up to 30 µL; the maximum concentration of Fe3+ in solution is 15 µM) was progressively introduced into the CDs solution within the quartz cuvette. The solution was well-mixed well before each measurement. The sensitivity of the CDs to Fe3+ ions was assessed by examining the variations in fluorescence intensity relative to Fe3+ ion concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) has been determined from the fluorescence quenching data using the equation 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity and K represents the slope of the linear plot.

To further validate the detection of Fe3+ ions in real aquatic environments, samples of tap water and pond water were taken from the university campus. Water samples were purified by filtration using a 0.22 µm filter membrane (Millipore) and subsequently subjected to centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 minutes. The stock solution of CDs was prepared in each water sample, with the absorbance maintained at approximately 0.2 at 340 nm. Utilizing the standard addition procedure, various concentrations of Fe3+ ion solution (1, 5, 10, and 15 µM) were mixed into each set of CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra of each solution have been observed using the above-described method. To enhance reliability, the Fe3+ ion detection tests were conducted in triplicate.

The CDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method utilizing palmyra seeds as the carbon source ( Scheme 1). Their morphology was examined using various microscopic techniques. The SEM image ( Fig. 1 a), captured at a 1 µm scale, reveals that the CDs exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1 b) at a 20 nm scale reveals that the CDs are monodisperse and exhibit a uniform size distribution. The mean particle size was determined to be ∼ 3.75 nm (Fig. 1 c). The Raman spectrum was measured to assess the crystallinity and graphitic characteristics of the carbon dots. Fig. 2 a illustrates the presence of two prominent peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands, respectively. The G band corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite, signifying sp2 carbon vibrations, while the D band denotes structural defects in sp3 hybridized carbon. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) is 0.87, signifying a moderate level of graphitization, influenced by functional groups that induce defects in the sp² carbon structure.

Additionally, the surface functional groups were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 b), indicating the presence of various surface functional groups. The prominent peak at 3265 cm−1 is attributed to N-H and O-H stretching, resulting from the presence of flavonoids in the germinated seed. The additional peaks at 2926, 2320, 1712, 1601, and 1044 cm−1 correspond to C-H, CC, COOH, CO, and C-O functional groups, respectively [31,32]. These results demonstrate a significant presence of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface, which enhances the stability and solubility of the CDs in aqueous environments.

The photophysical properties of the CDs were examined by UV-Vis absorption, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence techniques ( Fig. 3). The UV-Vis spectrum is represented in Fig. 3 a which shows that the fitted spectrum overlaps with the measured spectrum exhibiting a sharp peak at 278 nm which is attributed to the π-π* transition whereas the broad shoulder centered around 345 nm is attributed to the n-π* transitions in the CO bond, which is due to the presence of surface functional groups in the CDs. As per the literature [33], the prepared CDs also exhibit excitation dependent emissive characteristics as shown in Fig. 3 b. The emission maximum is centered around 430 nm at the excitation of 340 nm. It is evident that while varying the excitation wavelength at 20 nm intervals, a notable bathochromic shift accompanied by an increase in the fluorescence intensity till excitation of 340 nm followed by decrease in the emission intensity till the excitation of 460 nm. This phenomenon might be attributed to the intrinsic property of surface state, molecular state and quantum confinement effect. Furthermore, the quantum yield of the pristine CDs was determined using the relative method wherein quinine sulphate (0.01 M H2SO4; QY = 54 %) is used as the reference dye. The quantum yield was evaluated to be 12 %, which is comparable to that of the biomass-derived CDs [34]. Additionally, the excited state lifetime of the prepared CDs was measured using time correlated single photon counting technique. The CDs are excited at 390 nm and monitored at 430 nm (Fig. 3 c). The fluorescence decay curve was fitted using a tri-exponential function [F(t)=A1 exp(−t/τ1) + A2 exp(−t/τ2) + A3 exp(−t/ τ3)] with the lifetimes of τ1 = 3.71 ns (49 %), τ2 = 0.74 ns (20 %), and τ3 = 9.68 ns (31 %) and the average lifetime is calculated to be 4.71 ns using the formula τav = (A1τ1 + A2τ2 + A3τ3) / (A1 + A2 + A3). The ideal QY and nanosecond fluorescence lifetime of CDs provide them an attractive candidate for sensing, imaging, and related applications.

Despite the optimal quantum yield and lifetime rendering the palmyra-derived CDs as a promising choice for sensing applications, it remains essential to evaluate the stability of the CDs under various environmental conditions (Figure S1). Fig. 4 a depicts the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs within the pH range of 1–14. The CDs exhibit a consistent fluorescence intensity at a pH range of 5–9, but it diminishes under highly acidic (pH < 5) and alkaline (pH > 9) conditions. The presence of diverse surface functional groups in the generated CDs renders them prone to intramolecular hydrogen bonding and protonation/deprotonation, causing the reduction in fluorescence intensity under extreme pH conditions [35]. Additionally, the effects of saline conditions were evaluated using different concentrations of NaCl solution, as illustrated in Fig. 4 b. The CDs demonstrate no significant alteration in fluorescence intensity, even at elevated sodium ion concentrations. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 4 c, no significant alteration in fluorescence intensity was detected throughout a storage period of 90 days. The synthesized CDs have exceptional photostability, making them extremely versatile for practical applications

From the characterization section, it was concluded that the palmyra derived CDs display fine particle size, abundant functional groups, excellent hydrophilicity, good photoluminescence property and high photostability. These results instigate the CDs as a potential candidate for the detection of metal ions in an aqueous environment. Thus, the selectivity of the CDs towards diverse metal ions (Zn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, and Fe2+) were assessed at 10 µM concentration. The ions were subjected to the CDs solution and the fluorescent intensities were monitored at 430 nm. It was observed that Fe3+ ions exhibited a remarkable quenching efficiency whereas other metal ions had no apparent effect ( Fig. 5 a). This shows that the CDs have a high affinity towards Fe3+ ions due to the presence of phenolic group on their surface. Notably, the interference plot reveals that the CDs showed an excellent selectivity towards Fe3+ ions and no significant impact even in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 5 b). These findings demonstrate the potential of palmyra derived CDs as a prospective fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment.

To investigate the sensitivity of CDs towards Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium, a fluorimetric titration method has been employed. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs was monitored by varying the concentration of Fe3+ ions from the range of 0.3 µM – 15 µM. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the regular decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed with the increase in the concentration of the Fe3+ ions. In addition, there were no noticeable spectral shifts or peak broadening indicating the absence of excited state complexes. Fig. 6 b represents the plot between the change in fluorescence intensity with respect to the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection is calculated using the formula 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity and K represents the slope of the linear plot [36]. The linear range and LOD is evaluated to be 0.3 µM – 4 µM and 1.42 nM, respectively, which is better than several other biomass derived CDs (Table S1). Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer relationship was employed to determine the nature of quenching. A plot between the change in fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ ions yielded an upward curvature (Fig. 6 c). The upward curvature indicates the presence of static quenching which is typically attributed to the ground state interaction or complexation of the CDs with the Fe3+ ions [37]. Thus, to evaluate the association constant between the CDs and Fe3+ ions, modified Stern-Volmer relationship (
F
0
F
0
− F
=
1
f
a
K
SV
1
Q
+
1
f
a
), has been employed, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the presence and absence of the quencher, fa denotes the fraction of CDs available to the Fe3+ ions and KSV represents the modified Stern-Volmer constant. Fig. 6 d displays a plot between the F0/(F0–F) and 1/[Fe3+] and it exhibits a linear regression. The ratio of the intercept and slope is used to evaluate the association constant (Ka) which was found to be 1.87 × 104 M−1. This high magnitude of association constant is responsible for the superior LOD compared to other biomass derived-CDs (refer
Table 1) [38]. To understand the fluorescence quenching dynamics and exciton recombination process, TCSPC technique was employed. Fig. 7 a represents the fluorescence decay of CDs with and without the addition of Fe3+ ions which were excited at 390 nm and monitored at 430 nm. The decay of standalone CDs was fitted using a triexponential function which yielded an average lifetime of 4.71 ns. Whereas the fluorescence decay of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions was fitted using biexponential function [F(t)=A1 exp(−t/τ1) + A2 exp(−t/τ2)] with the lifetimes of τ1 = 0.9 ns (87 %) and τ2 = 2.83 ns (13 %). The average fluorescence lifetime was estimated to be 1.15 ns using the formula τav = (A1τ1 + A2τ2)/ (A1 + A2). The significant decrease in the average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs with the addition of Fe3+ ions indicates that the excited state electron transfer from the CDs to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions is responsible for the fluorescence quenching [39,40]. This concludes that the mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching is the oxidative electron transfer in the excited state of CD-Fe3+ system (Fig. 7 b).

Ferritin is a crucial iron storage protein that regulates iron homeostasis by storing excess iron and releasing it as needed. Its hollow spherical configuration can accommodate 4500 iron atoms. Free iron produces reactive oxygen species (ROS), resulting in oxidative stress and cellular damage. Consequently, a dynamic equilibrium between ferritin and iron is meticulously regulated, rendering ferritin a crucial biomarker for the diagnosis of iron-related disorders, including anemia, hemochromatosis, and inflammatory diseases [45,46]. Monitoring ferritin levels is essential for evaluating iron metabolism, making it highly significant in the clinical sector. The selectivity of the CDs towards various proteins such as ferritin, human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), myoglobin, and lysozyme were investigated at pH 6. The pH is maintained at 6 due to the inability of ferritin to release Fe3+ ions at physiological pH [47].

Fig. 8 a indicates that the ferritin demonstrates exceptional quenching efficiency compared to the other protein which can be attributed to the higher amount of Fe3+ ions stored within the ferritin shell. As shown in Fig. 8 b, a regular decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed with the increase in the ferritin concentration. CDs exhibit a linear range of 0.3–3 µg/ml in the detection of ferritin as shown in Fig. 8 c. Furthermore, the LOD was determined to be 0.021 µg/ml based on the linear relationship observed in the plot, which is comparable with the previously reported values for fluorimetric ferritin detection [40,48–50]. Graphical illustration of the possible interaction between the CDs and ferritin is depicted in Scheme 2. The CDs selectively bind to the Fe³ ⁺ ions stored within the ferritin protein shell, leading to fluorescence quenching. This interaction is facilitated by the surface functional groups of the CDs, such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups, which specifically recognize and bind to the Fe3+ ions in ferritin. These results demonstrate the prospective potential of the palmyra derived CDs for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in ferritin protein.

All the experiments rendered the CDs as a potent candidate for practical applications. Thus, the efficacy of CDs for the practical detection of Fe3+ ions has been verified utilizing tap water and pond water samples. In order to minimize errors in the experiment, the pH of both water solutions was calibrated to pH = 7. The obtained water samples were examined for Fe3+ ions using CDs; however, no Fe3+ ions were detected in either sample. Consequently, the specified concentrations of Fe3+ ions (1, 5, 10, and 15 µM) were spiked into both water samples. The fluorescence intensity of CDs with added Fe3+ ions was measured. The results are enumerated in Table 2. The recovery of Fe3+ is satisfactory, ranging from 99 % to 101 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 0.79 %, demonstrating the feasibility and dependability of CDs as an effective fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection in aqueous environments.

The fluorescent carbon dots were effectively developed from palmyra seeds using a one-step hydrothermal method without including any specific chemicals. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics with a quantum yield of 12 %. The CDs display exceptional fluorescence stability within the pH range of 5–9 and demonstrate significant resistance to ionic strength. The CDs demonstrate exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, as well as rapid detection of Fe3+ ions by a synergistic mechanism including static fluorescence quenching and photoinduced electron transfer, attaining a linear range of 0.3–4 µM with a limit of detection of 1.42 nM. The synthesized CDs can effectively detect Fe3+ ions in tap and pond water, achieving a recovery of 100 % and an RSD of less than 0.79 %. Furthermore, the CDs were employed to identify ferritin within a linear range of 0.3–3 µg/ml. Overall, the synthesized carbon compound will serve as a promising fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in aquatic environments.

Kathiravan Arunkumar: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ilango Bhuvaneesh: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Asha Jhonsi Mariadoss: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financialinterestsor personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.K. would like to express gratitude to Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr. Sagunthala R & D Institute of Science and Technology for the SEED research grant (VTU/Seed Fund/FY 2023–2024/014). A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2025.111862.

Sustain Oper. Comput.
